title: Punta Arenas

Panoramic view of downtown Punta Arenas, from La Cruz Hills  **Second**: Sara Braun Palace ([Palacio Sara Braun](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Palacio_Sara_Braun "es:Palacio Sara Braun")), Ferdinand Magellan Monument in Muñoz Gamero Square ([Plaza Muñoz Gamero](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plaza_Mu%C3%B1oz_Gamero "es:Plaza Muñoz Gamero"))  **Third**: [Punta Arenas Sacred Heart Cathedral](/wiki/Sacred_Heart_Cathedral,_Punta_Arenas "Sacred Heart Cathedral, Punta Arenas"), [Cemetery of Punta Arenas](/wiki/Cemetery_of_Punta_Arenas "Cemetery of Punta Arenas")  **Fourth**: Shepherd Monument, Goleta Ancud Monument  **Bottom**: An overview of [Strait of Magellan](/wiki/Strait_of_Magellan "Strait of Magellan"), from Costanera area | |

(Spanish pronunciation: [[ˈpunta aˈɾenas]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish"), historically known as **Sandy Point** in English) is the capital [city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Chile "List of cities in Chile") of [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile")'s southernmost [region](/wiki/Regions_of_Chile "Regions of Chile"), [Magallanes and Antarctica Chilena](/wiki/Magallanes_Region "Magallanes Region"). Although the city officially was renamed **Magallanes** in 1927, its name was changed back to Punta Arenas in 1938. The city is the largest south of the [46th parallel south](/wiki/46th_parallel_south "46th parallel south") and the most populous southernmost [city in Chile](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Chile "List of cities in Chile") and the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"). Due to its location, it is also the coldest coastal city with more than 100,000 inhabitants in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"). Punta Arenas is one of the world's most southerly ports and serves as an [Antarctic gateway city](/wiki/Antarctic_Gateway_Cities "Antarctic Gateway Cities"). Punta Arenas is the world's southernmost city with more than 100,000 inhabitants and claims the title of southernmost city in the world, although that title is also claimed by [Ushuaia](/wiki/Ushuaia "Ushuaia") in Argentina, which lies farther south but is slightly smaller than Punta Arenas.

**Punta Arenas** (Spanish pronunciation: [[ˈpunta aˈɾenas]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish"), historically known as **Sandy Point** in English) is the capital [city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Chile "List of cities in Chile") of [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile")'s southernmost [region](/wiki/Regions_of_Chile "Regions of Chile"), [Magallanes and Antarctica Chilena](/wiki/Magallanes_Region "Magallanes Region"). Although the city officially was renamed **Magallanes** in 1927, its name was changed back to Punta Arenas in 1938. The city is the largest south of the [46th parallel south](/wiki/46th_parallel_south "46th parallel south") and the most populous southernmost [city in Chile](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Chile "List of cities in Chile") and the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"). Due to its location, it is also the coldest coastal city with more than 100,000 inhabitants in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"). Punta Arenas is one of the world's most southerly ports and serves as an [Antarctic gateway city](/wiki/Antarctic_Gateway_Cities "Antarctic Gateway Cities"). Punta Arenas is the world's southernmost city with more than 100,000 inhabitants and claims the title of southernmost city in the world, although that title is also claimed by [Ushuaia](/wiki/Ushuaia "Ushuaia") in Argentina, which lies farther south but is slightly smaller than Punta Arenas.

Since 1977, Punta Arenas has been one of only two [free ports](/wiki/List_of_free_ports "List of free ports") in Chile, the other being [Iquique](/wiki/Iquique "Iquique") in the country's far north.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[A]](#cite_note-5) Located on the [Brunswick Peninsula](/wiki/Brunswick_Peninsula "Brunswick Peninsula") north of the [Strait of Magellan](/wiki/Strait_of_Magellan "Strait of Magellan"), Punta Arenas was originally established by the Chilean government in 1848 as a small [penal colony](/wiki/Penal_colony "Penal colony") to assert sovereignty over the Strait. During the remainder of the 19th century, Punta Arenas grew in size and importance due to increasing maritime traffic to the west coasts of South and North America. The city's growth was also due to waves of European immigrants, mainly from [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") and [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), attracted by a [gold rush](/wiki/Tierra_del_Fuego_gold_rush "Tierra del Fuego gold rush"), and [sheep farming boom](/wiki/Patagonian_sheep_farming_boom "Patagonian sheep farming boom") in the 1880s and early 20th century. The largest sheep company, which controlled 10,000 square kilometres in Chile and Argentina, was based in Punta Arenas, and its owners lived there.

Since its founding, Chile has used Punta Arenas as a base to defend its sovereignty claims in the southernmost part of South America. That led to the Strait of Magellan being recognized as Chilean territory in the [Boundary Treaty of 1881 between Chile and Argentina](/wiki/Boundary_Treaty_of_1881_between_Chile_and_Argentina "Boundary Treaty of 1881 between Chile and Argentina"). Punta Arenas' geopolitical importance has remained high in the 20th and 21st centuries due to its logistic significance in accessing the [Antarctic Peninsula](/wiki/Antarctic_Peninsula "Antarctic Peninsula").

Since 2017, the city and its region have been on their [own time zone](/wiki/Time_in_Chile#Time_zones "Time in Chile"), using summer time throughout the year (UTC−3). The city is supplied with water from the [San Juan River](/wiki/San_Juan_River_(Chile) "San Juan River (Chile)").[[5]](#cite_note-BCNhidro-6)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punta_Arenas&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name *Punta Arenas* whose literal translation would be "Point Sands", is actually derived from the Spanish term *Punta Arenosa*, a literal translation of the English name "Sandy Point".

The name Sandy Point derives from the [voyage of John Narborough](/wiki/English_expedition_to_Valdivia "English expedition to Valdivia") in 1669-1671. He wrote in his account:

> Sand-Point [sic] is a mean low Point, lies out more than the other Points of the shore, and a few trees grow on it.[[6]](#cite_note-7)

The English 18th-century explorer [John Byron](/wiki/John_Byron "John Byron") is sometimes erroneously credited with naming the area.

The city has also been known as *Magallanes*. Today that term is normally used to describe the administrative region which includes the city.

Punta Arenas has been nicknamed "the city of the red roofs" for the red-painted metal roofs that characterized the city for many years. Since about 1970, the availability of other colours in protective finishes has resulted in greater variety in the characteristic metal roofs.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punta_Arenas&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

Located on the [Brunswick Peninsula](/wiki/Brunswick_Peninsula "Brunswick Peninsula"), Punta Arenas is among the largest cities in [Patagonia](/wiki/Patagonia "Patagonia"). In 2012, it had a population of 127,454.[[7]](#cite_note-8) It is roughly 1,419 km (882 mi) from the coast of Antarctica and 635 km (395 mi) from [Ushuaia](/wiki/Ushuaia "Ushuaia"), the capital of the Argentine province of [Tierra Del Fuego](/wiki/Tierra_del_Fuego_Province,_Argentina "Tierra del Fuego Province, Argentina").

The Magallanes region is considered part of Chilean [Patagonia](/wiki/Patagonia "Patagonia"). Magallanes is Spanish for [Magellan](/wiki/Ferdinand_Magellan "Ferdinand Magellan"), and was named after Ferdinand Magellan, the Portuguese explorer sailing for [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"). While circumnavigating the Earth for Spain, he passed close to the present site of Punta Arenas in 1520. Early English navigational documents referred to this site as "Sandy Point."

The city proper is located on the north-eastern shore of Brunswick Peninsula. Except for the eastern shore, containing the settlements of Guairabo, Rio Amarillo and Punta San Juan, the peninsula is largely uninhabited. The municipality of Punta Arenas includes all of Brunswick Peninsula, as well as all islands west of the *[Isla Grande de Tierra del Fuego](/wiki/Tierra_del_Fuego_(main_island) "Tierra del Fuego (main island)")* and north of [Cockburn](/wiki/Cockburn_Channel "Cockburn Channel") and [Magdalena](/wiki/Magdalena_Channel "Magdalena Channel") channels.

The largest of those are:

* [Santa Inés Island](/wiki/Santa_In%C3%A9s_Island "Santa Inés Island")
* [Desolación Island](/wiki/Desolaci%C3%B3n_Island "Desolación Island")
* [Dawson Island](/wiki/Dawson_Island "Dawson Island")
* [Aracena Island](/wiki/Aracena_Island "Aracena Island")
* [Clarence Island](/wiki/Clarence_Island,_Chile "Clarence Island, Chile")
* *Carlos Island*
* *Wickham Island*

Except [Dawson Island](/wiki/Dawson_Island "Dawson Island"), which had a population of about 301 in 2002, the islands are largely uninhabited. [Clarence Island](/wiki/Clarence_Island,_Chile "Clarence Island, Chile") had a population of five.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punta_Arenas&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Punta_Arenas_con_nieve.jpeg/250px-Punta_Arenas_con_nieve.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Punta_Arenas_con_nieve.jpeg)

Snow in winter, 2014

Due to its far southern latitude, Punta Arenas has a [subpolar oceanic climate](/wiki/Subpolar_oceanic_climate "Subpolar oceanic climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Cfc*, [Trewartha climate classification](/wiki/Trewartha_climate_classification "Trewartha climate classification") *Eolk*). The seasonal temperature in Punta Arenas is greatly moderated by its proximity to the ocean, with average lows in July near −1 °C (30 °F) and highs in January of 14 °C (57 °F).

It is known for stable constant temperatures, which vary only slightly with the seasons. Rainfall is highest in April and May, and the snowy season runs throughout the Chilean winter (June until September). As in most of [Patagonia](/wiki/Patagonia "Patagonia"), average annual precipitation is quite low, only 380 mm (15 in), because of a [rain shadow](/wiki/Rain_shadow "Rain shadow") created by the Andes. The average temperature does not go below 1 °C (34 °F).[[8]](#cite_note-Punta_Arenas_article-9) The city is also known for its high winds (up to 130 km/h [81 mph]), which are strongest during the summer. City officials have put up ropes between buildings in the downtown area to assist pedestrians with managing the strong downdrafts.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After 1986, Punta Arenas became the first significantly populated city in the world to be affected directly by the thinning [ozone layer](/wiki/Ozone_layer "Ozone layer"). Its residents are considered to be exposed to potentially damaging levels of [ultraviolet radiation](/wiki/Ultraviolet_radiation "Ultraviolet radiation").[[9]](#cite_note-NYT03Mar95-10)[[10]](#cite_note-PPPv18i6-11)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punta_Arenas&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Spanish colonization attempt of the Strait of Magellan](/wiki/Spanish_colonization_attempt_of_the_Strait_of_Magellan "Spanish colonization attempt of the Strait of Magellan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Casa_Espa%C3%B1a%2C_Punta_Arenas%2C_Magallanes%2C_Chile.jpg/250px-Casa_Espa%C3%B1a%2C_Punta_Arenas%2C_Magallanes%2C_Chile.jpg)](/wiki/File:Casa_Espa%C3%B1a,_Punta_Arenas,_Magallanes,_Chile.jpg)

Casa España, established in 1917 by the *Spanish Society of Socorros Mutuos*. It was formed between 1936 and 1938. Plaza de Armas ([Muñoz Gamero Park](/w/index.php?title=Mu%C3%B1oz_Gamero_Park&action=edit&redlink=1 "Muñoz Gamero Park (page does not exist)")).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Avenida_Crist%C3%B3bal_Col%C3%B3n%2C_Punta_Arenas%2C_Magallanes%2C_Chile.jpg/250px-Avenida_Crist%C3%B3bal_Col%C3%B3n%2C_Punta_Arenas%2C_Magallanes%2C_Chile.jpg)](/wiki/File:Avenida_Crist%C3%B3bal_Col%C3%B3n,_Punta_Arenas,_Magallanes,_Chile.jpg)

Christopher Columbus Avenue, the location of the Old Machine House, built in 1890 by Croatians, 2017

Two early [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_Chilean "Spanish Chilean") settlements were attempted along this coast (on the Straits of Magellan). The first was founded in 1584 and was called *Nombre de Jesús.* It failed due to the harsh weather and difficulty in the settlers' obtaining food and water, and the enormous distances from other Spanish ports. A second colony, *Ciudad del Rey don Felipe*, was attempted about 80 kilometres south of Punta Arenas. This became known later as *[Puerto del Hambre](/wiki/Puerto_del_Hambre "Puerto del Hambre")*, which translates to *Port Famine*. Spain had established these settlements in an attempt to protect its shipping and prevent piracy by [English](/wiki/English_people "English people") [pirates](/wiki/Pirate "Pirate"), by controlling the Straits of Magellan. An English [privateer](/wiki/Privateer "Privateer"), [Thomas Cavendish](/wiki/Thomas_Cavendish "Thomas Cavendish"), during his [circumnavigation](/wiki/Thomas_Cavendish%27s_circumnavigation "Thomas Cavendish's circumnavigation"), rescued the last surviving member of Puerto del Hambre in 1587.[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18)

### Penal colony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punta_Arenas&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Penal colony")]

Further information: [Chilean colonization of the Strait of Magellan](/wiki/Chilean_colonization_of_the_Strait_of_Magellan "Chilean colonization of the Strait of Magellan")

In 1843 the [Chilean government](/wiki/Chilean_government "Chilean government") sent an expedition to build a fort and establish a permanent settlement on the shores of the Strait of Magellan. It built and commissioned a schooner called *Goleta Ancud* which, under the command of [John Williams Wilson](/wiki/John_Williams_Wilson "John Williams Wilson") of the Chilean Navy, transported a crew of 21 people ([captain](/wiki/Captain_(naval) "Captain (naval)"), eighteen crew, and two women), plus cargo, to accomplish the mandate. The founding act of the settlement took place on 21 September 1843.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

The fort was well-positioned on a small rocky [peninsula](/wiki/Peninsula "Peninsula"), but the location could not support a proper civilian settlement. With that in mind, in 1848, the military governor, José de los Santos Mardones, decided to move the settlement to its current location, along the *Las Minas* river, and renamed it Punta Arenas.

In the mid-19th century, Chile used Punta Arenas as a penal colony and a disciplinary posting for military personnel with "problematic" behaviour. It also settled immigrants there. In December 1851, a prisoners' [mutiny led by Lieutenant Cambiaso](/wiki/Mutiny_of_Cambiazo "Mutiny of Cambiazo"), resulted in the murder of Governor Muñoz Gamero and the resident priest, and the destruction of the church and the hospital.[[19]](#cite_note-20) The mutiny was put down by [Commander Stewart](/wiki/William_Houston_Stewart "William Houston Stewart") of [HMS *Virago*](/wiki/HMS_Virago_(1842) "HMS Virago (1842)") assisted by two Chilean ships: *Indefatigable* and *[Meteoro](/wiki/Meteoro_(brigantine) "Meteoro (brigantine)")*.[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22)

In 1867, President [José Joaquín Pérez](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Joaqu%C3%ADn_P%C3%A9rez "José Joaquín Pérez") issued a decree offering land grants in an effort to get Chileans or foreigners to settle around Punta Arenas. The first British immigrants arrived in 1867, and their number increased as [sheep farming](/wiki/Sheep_farming "Sheep farming") grew in the [Chilean Magallanes](/wiki/Magallanes_Region "Magallanes Region"). The greatest immigration continued to be by the British until 1906, when Croatians surpassed them in numbers.[[22]](#cite_note-William-23)

An 1877 mutiny, known as *El motín de los artilleros* (Mutiny of the Artillerymen), led to the destruction of a large part of the town and the murder of many civilians not directly associated with the prison. In time the city was restored. The growth of the sheep farming industry and the [discovery of gold](/wiki/Tierra_del_Fuego_Gold_Rush "Tierra del Fuego Gold Rush"), as well as increasing trade via sailing ships, attracted many new settlers, and the town began to prosper.

### Economic boom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punta_Arenas&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Economic boom")]

Between about 1890 and 1940, the [Magallanes region](/wiki/Magallanes_y_la_Ant%C3%A1rtica_Chilena_Region "Magallanes y la Antártica Chilena Region") became an important sheep-raising region, with one company (*[Sociedad Explotadora de Tierra del Fuego](/wiki/Sociedad_Explotadora_de_Tierra_del_Fuego "Sociedad Explotadora de Tierra del Fuego")*) controlling over 11,000 square kilometres (4,200 sq mi). In 1910, Sociedad Explotadora merged with Sociedad Ganadera, resulting in a company possessing 3 million [hectares](/wiki/Hectare "Hectare") in southern Chile and Argentina, with over two million head of sheep.[[22]](#cite_note-William-23): 120–125 The headquarters of this company and the residences of the owners were in Punta Arenas. The Sarah Braun Museum is now established at the former Braun-Menéndez mansion, in the centre of Punta Arenas.

The Punta Arenas harbour, although exposed to storms, was considered one of the most important in Chile before the construction of the [Panama Canal](/wiki/Panama_Canal "Panama Canal"). It was used as a [coaling station](/wiki/Coaling_station "Coaling station") by the [steamships](/wiki/Steamships "Steamships") transiting between the Atlantic and Pacific oceans. Today it is mostly used by tourism cruises and scientific expeditions.

### Modern city

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punta_Arenas&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Modern city")]

## Demography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punta_Arenas&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Demography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Vista_Sur.jpg/250px-Vista_Sur.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vista_Sur.jpg)

The *[County of Peebles](/wiki/County_of_Peebles_(ship) "County of Peebles (ship)")* and *Cavenga* are used as a [breakwater](/wiki/Breakwater_(structure) "Breakwater (structure)") for the harbour at Punta Arenas, 2023

In 2012 Punta Arenas recorded a population of over 127,000 inhabitants for the (2012 Census) by the [National Statistics Institute](/wiki/National_Statistics_Institute_(Chile) "National Statistics Institute (Chile)"). The population grew by 5.1% (5,830 persons) between the 1992 and 2002 censuses and further rose to 127,454 at the 2012 Census.[[3]](#cite_note-INE-3)

The city was populated by many colonists from [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") and [Croatia](/wiki/Croatian_Chilean "Croatian Chilean") in the mid-nineteenth century, and many of their descendants remain. Other national ethnic groups represented are [German](/wiki/German_Chilean "German Chilean"), [English](/wiki/English_Chilean "English Chilean"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_Chilean "Italian Chilean"), [Swiss](/wiki/Swiss_Chilean "Swiss Chilean") and [Irish](/wiki/Irish_Chilean "Irish Chilean").

[Croatian immigration](/wiki/Croatian_people "Croatian people") to Punta Arenas was a crucial development in the region of [Magallanes](/wiki/Magallanes_y_la_Ant%C3%A1rtica_Chilena_Region "Magallanes y la Antártica Chilena Region") and the city in particular. Currently, this influence is still reflected in the names of shops, streets and many buildings. Punta Arenas is said to have the largest percentage of Croatians in the world outside Croatia and the former Yugoslavia.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Punta Arenas also has the largest percentage of residents of British descent in the whole of Chile.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Punta Arenas is home to the southernmost Hindu temple in the world, which is used by the relatively small, but significant, [Sindhi](/wiki/Sindhis "Sindhis") community in Punta Arenas. Sindhi merchants began arriving in the area during the early 1900s, and today constitute one of the largest communities of [Indians in Chile](/wiki/Indians_in_Chile "Indians in Chile").[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punta_Arenas&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Laurence_M_Gould.JPG/250px-Laurence_M_Gould.JPG)](/wiki/File:Laurence_M_Gould.JPG)

Ice breaker [RV *Laurence M. Gould*](/wiki/RV_Laurence_M._Gould "RV Laurence M. Gould") in Punta Arenas. Punta Arenas is an important point in the supply of [Antarctic bases](/wiki/Research_stations_in_Antarctica "Research stations in Antarctica") in [West Antarctica](/wiki/West_Antarctica "West Antarctica"), 2006

By 2006 the economy of Punta Arenas and the region had diversified. Chile's principal oil reserves are close by, along with some [low-grade coal](/wiki/Coal_mining_in_Chile "Coal mining in Chile").[[26]](#cite_note-27)[[27]](#cite_note-Worldcoal-28)

Agricultural production, including sheep and cattle, continues to play a significant role.

Tourism has contributed to the city's economy and steady growth. Tourist destinations include the Cathedral and other notable churches, the city cemetery, and the statue of Magellan. Some cruise ships to Antarctica depart from Punta Arenas's port, which also serves as a hub for many cruise lines that travel along the channels and fjords of the region.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A scheduled ferry service connects Punta Arenas with the main island of [Tierra del Fuego](/wiki/Tierra_del_Fuego "Tierra del Fuego"), and a less frequent ferry runs to the Chilean town of [Puerto Williams](/wiki/Puerto_Williams "Puerto Williams").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## International relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punta_Arenas&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: International relations")]

The city of Punta Arenas hosts a number of international relations institutions, such as the Regional Unit of International Affairs (URAI) of the Regional Government of Magallanes and Chilean Antarctica, responsible for analyzing and managing the region's bilateral and multilateral relations with Latin America and the rest of the world; the Tourism and International Relations Commission of the Regional Council of Magallanes and Chilean Antarctica; the regional office of the [National Migration Service](/w/index.php?title=National_Migration_Service&action=edit&redlink=1 "National Migration Service (page does not exist)"); the regional office of the [General Directorate for Export Promotion](/w/index.php?title=General_Directorate_for_Export_Promotion&action=edit&redlink=1 "General Directorate for Export Promotion (page does not exist)") (ProChile); and the Department of Migration and International Police of the [Investigations Police](/wiki/Investigations_Police_of_Chile "Investigations Police of Chile").[[28]](#cite_note-29)
In the field of higher education internationalization, the main actor in Punta Arenas is the Directorate of International Relations of the [University of Magallanes](/wiki/University_of_Magallanes "University of Magallanes").[[29]](#cite_note-30)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punta_Arenas&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Education")]

[University of Magallanes](/wiki/University_of_Magallanes "University of Magallanes") (UMAG) is in the southern [Chilean](/wiki/Chile "Chile") city of Punta Arenas. It is part of the Chilean Traditional Universities. The University of Magallanes was established in 1981 during the [neoliberal](/wiki/Neoliberal "Neoliberal") reforms of Chile's [military regime](/wiki/Military_regime "Military regime") as the successor of [Universidad Técnica del Estado](/wiki/University_of_Santiago,_Chile#Universidad_Técnica_del_Estado "University of Santiago, Chile")'s Punta Arenas section. Universidad Técnica del Estado had established the Punta Arenas section in 1961.

The University of Magallanes has campuses in Punta Arenas and [Puerto Natales](/wiki/Puerto_Natales "Puerto Natales") as well as a university centre in [Puerto Williams](/wiki/Puerto_Williams "Puerto Williams"). University of Magallanes publishes the humanities and social sciences journal *[Magallania](/wiki/Magallania "Magallania")* twice a year.

There is a German school, Deutsche Schule Punta Arenas.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

## Culture

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punta_Arenas&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Museums")]

* **Braun Menéndez Regional Museum** ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Museo Regional Braun Menéndez*)

The museum is located in the Braun Menéndez Palace, which is a National Monument. Since February 1983, the "Magallanes Regional Museum" has been operating here, which has material from the contemporary history of the region.

* **Salesian Regional Museum Maggiorino Borgatello** ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Museo Regional Salesiano Maggiorino Borgatello*)

The museum is located at Avenida Bulnes no. 374, next to the Santuario María Auxiliadora. It is the most important in the area, and has a complete collection of species from the region and the [Selkʼnam](/wiki/Selk%CA%BCnam_people "Selkʼnam people") culture. Samples from [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica") are also on display.
It was created in 1893 by the [Salesians](/wiki/Salesians_of_Don_Bosco "Salesians of Don Bosco") religious congregation, and is maintained by voluntary contributions made by the community.

* **Naval and Maritime Museum of Punta Arenas** ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Museo Naval y Marítimo de Punta Arenas*)

The museum is located at Pedro Montt No. 981, next to the Military Museum. It holds a historical collection of the [Chilean Armed Forces](/wiki/Chilean_Armed_Forces "Chilean Armed Forces") during the colonization of the territory of [Magallanes and Chilean Antarctica](/wiki/Magallanes_Region "Magallanes Region").

* **Nao *Victoria* Museum** ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Museo Nao Victoria*)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/NaoVictoria.JPG/250px-NaoVictoria.JPG)](/wiki/File:NaoVictoria.JPG)

[*Nao Victoria*](/wiki/Victoria_(ship) "Victoria (ship)"), Magellan's ship replica, in the [Museo Nao Victoria](/wiki/Museo_Nao_Victoria "Museo Nao Victoria") Punta Arenas, 2011

Main article: [Museo Nao Victoria (Chile)](/wiki/Museo_Nao_Victoria_(Chile) "Museo Nao Victoria (Chile)")

The museum is located 7.5 km north on Route Y-565 to Rio Seco. This museum exhibits a full-size replica of the first ship ever to circumnavigate the world: Ferdinand Magellan's *[Nao Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(ship) "Victoria (ship)").*[[31]](#cite_note-32) Since October 2011, the museum has displayed a full-size replica of the *[James Caird](/wiki/Voyage_of_the_James_Caird "Voyage of the James Caird")*, used by [Ernest Shackleton](/wiki/Ernest_Shackleton "Ernest Shackleton") during his [Imperial Trans-Antarctic Expedition](/wiki/Imperial_Trans-Antarctic_Expedition "Imperial Trans-Antarctic Expedition") with the [*Endurance*](/wiki/Endurance_(1912_ship) "Endurance (1912 ship)").

* **Museum of Remembrance** ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Museo del Recuerdo*)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Carreta_Museo_del_Recuerdo.JPG/250px-Carreta_Museo_del_Recuerdo.JPG)](/wiki/File:Carreta_Museo_del_Recuerdo.JPG)

Museum of Remembrance, 2010

The Museum of Remembrance of the [University of Magallanes](/wiki/University_of_Magallanes "University of Magallanes") Instituto de la Patagonia displays examples of heritage buildings, old machinery and tools. They are part of an Open Museum, with high attendance and regional identity and a significant number of foreign visitors, especially during the spring, summer and autumn seasons. It has 8 heritage wooden buildings; 40 antique vehicles and transport of great historical value; machines; and innumerable tools, mainly associated with livestock and oil production.

## Administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punta_Arenas&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Administration")]

As a "comuna" (commune) Punta Arenas is a third-level [administrative division of Chile](/wiki/Administrative_division_of_Chile "Administrative division of Chile") administered by a [municipal council](/wiki/Municipal_council "Municipal council"), headed by an [alcalde](/wiki/Alcalde "Alcalde") who is directly elected every four years. The 2016–2020 alcalde is Claudio Radonich ([National Renewal](/wiki/National_Renewal_(Chile) "National Renewal (Chile)")).[[1]](#cite_note-Munitel-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Official-2)

Within the [electoral divisions of Chile](/wiki/Electoral_divisions_of_Chile "Electoral divisions of Chile"), Punta Arenas is represented in the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_of_Chile "Chamber of Deputies of Chile") by Sandra Amar ([UDI](/wiki/Independent_Democratic_Union "Independent Democratic Union")), Karim Bianchi (IND-[PRSD](/wiki/Radical_Party_of_Chile "Radical Party of Chile")) and Gabriel Boric ([CS](/wiki/Social_Convergence "Social Convergence")) as part of the 60th electoral district, which includes the entire [Magallanes and Antartica Chilena Region](/wiki/Magallanes_and_Antartica_Chilena_Region "Magallanes and Antartica Chilena Region"). The commune is represented in the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Chile "Senate of Chile") by Carlos Bianchi Chelech (Ind.) and Carolina Goic B. ([DC](/wiki/Christian_Democratic_Party_(Chile) "Christian Democratic Party (Chile)")) as part of the 19th senatorial constituency (Magallanes Region).

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Punta_Arenas&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Comuna_de_Punta_Arenas.svg/250px-Comuna_de_Punta_Arenas.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Comuna_de_Punta_Arenas.svg)

Map of the Punta Arenas commune in Magallanes Region

[Carlos Ibáñez del Campo](/wiki/Presidente_Carlos_Ib%C3%A1%C3%B1ez_del_Campo_International_Airport "Presidente Carlos Ibáñez del Campo International Airport") airport is located 20 kilometers from the city centre. Rental car services, [duty-free shops](/wiki/Duty-free_shop "Duty-free shop") and custom office services are available in the building (there is no duty-free shop in the terminal, despite the representations of tourist literature). Airlines serving the airport include [LATAM Chile](/wiki/LATAM_Chile "LATAM Chile") and [Sky Airline](/wiki/Sky_Airline "Sky Airline"), as well as charter flights. The civilian airport and the military airport make up the larger complex.

The city has sea, land, and air connections. By road, a connection to other regions of Chile requires passing through Argentine territory.[[8]](#cite_note-Punta_Arenas_article-9) By sea, several cruises and ferries can take visitors to the city, although the costs are higher because they include stops at tourist sites along the route.
